It is: **build a very small supervised governance tool on Colossus that writes explicit records and uses the local model only where helpful.**
Minimum Viable PEM Deployment On Colossus**
Minimum viable PEM should be a **local CLI tool plus file-backed state**, running beside CCE.

every ticket has a case id

Which Existing PEM Capabilities Are Actually Required**
Using the repo’s documented PEM role, these are the only capabilities I think are truly required at first:
one case record per ticket/work item
record source files, commands run, outputs, notes, and decision rationale
JSON or Markdown records stored on disk, human-readable and Git-friendly
That is the minimum useful PEM.
Which PEM Capabilities Can Be Deferred**
automatic linking from Matrix messages into PEM
using PEM to govern every tiny interaction
any attempt to make PEM the “brain” of Colossus
At first, PEM should be more like a **disciplined case notebook with operator commands**.
What Local-Model Support Is Required From Colossus**
**At least one usable local model for structured text work**
PEM must work even if the model is unavailable
the model should assist, not be required for basic governance

turns.answer:
That output is actually very helpful.

It means:

- `rsync` worked
- your Mac can compare the local CCE folder to the Colossus CCE folder
- only **2 files** currently look different

Those 2 files are:

- `infra/matrix/state/postgres/pg_wal/00000001000000000000000E`
- `runtime/matrix-sync-state.json`

Here’s the important part: those are **not normal code files**.

They are more like “live machine state” files:
- `pg_wal/...` = PostgreSQL internal database write-ahead log
- `runtime/matrix-sync-state.json` = CCE’s live Matrix listener cursor/state

ELI-5:
- these are not the recipe
- these are the crumbs on the kitchen floor while the app is running

So this is why we do a dry run first: it showed us that a naive “copy everything” command is too blunt.

We do **not** want our normal sync command to push or delete:
- live database state
- runtime state
- other machine-specific junk

So the next step is: **make a safer rsync command**.

Use this dry run next:

```bash
rsync -av --delete --dry-run \
  --exclude 'runtime/' \
  --exclude 'infra/matrix/state/' \
  --exclude '.git/' \
  /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce/ \
  colossus:/Users/kevinmattice/cce/
```

What these excludes mean:
- `runtime/` = don’t copy live bot state
- `infra/matrix/state/` = don’t copy live database/server state
- `.git/` = don’t copy Git internals this way

This is much closer to the safe workflow we want.

Please run that exact command and paste the output. If it comes back with little or nothing, that’s good news.

turns.answer:
That tells us the answer: **this local repo has no GitHub remote configured yet**.

So Git isn’t refusing to push. It just doesn’t know **where** to push.

Think of it like this:
- your work is packed and ready
- but Git doesn’t have the mailing address yet

Because you previously mentioned [`kevinmattice/cognitive-environment`](https://github.com/kevinmattice/cognitive-environment), I’m assuming that is the GitHub repo you want this local repo connected to.

If that assumption is right, here’s the simplest path.

**Step 1: add the GitHub address**
Run this:

```bash
cd /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce
git remote add origin git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git
```

What this means:
- `origin` = nickname for your GitHub repo
- `git@github.com:...` = the SSH address of the repo

**Step 2: verify it stuck**
Then run:

```bash
git remote -v
```

You should now see something like:

```bash
origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (fetch)
origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (push)
```

**Step 3: push your local `master`**
If Step 2 looks right, run:

```bash
git push -u origin master
```

What this means:
- `push` = send your commits to GitHub
- `-u` = remember this remote/branch pairing so future pushes are easier
- `master` = your current branch

After that, future pushes are usually just:

```bash
git push
```

**One small caution**
This will work only if your Mac already has GitHub SSH access set up.

If it does not, GitHub may say something like:
- `Permission denied (publickey)`

If that happens, don’t worry. That would mean:
- the repo address is correct
- but your Mac hasn’t authenticated to GitHub yet

So let’s do this one step at a time.

Please run just these first two:

```bash
cd /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce
git remote add origin git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git
git remote -v
```

turns.answer:
Below is the shortest practical path I’d recommend from today’s CCE Lite state to a usable **PEM-on-Colossus** workflow.

The key reality check is this:

- **CCE Lite is real and working**
- **PEM is not implemented yet**
- the repo currently has **PEM role docs and case artifacts**, not a functioning PEM runtime

So the fastest path is not “finish PEM architecture.”  
It is: **build a very small supervised governance tool on Colossus that writes explicit records and uses the local model only where helpful.**

**1. Minimum Viable PEM Deployment On Colossus**

Minimum viable PEM should be a **local CLI tool plus file-backed state**, running beside CCE.

Smallest deployable form:
- a `pem/` tool directory on Colossus
- one CLI entrypoint, for example `pemctl`
- one durable state area, likely:
  - `cases/` for case records
  - `scratch/` for temporary working material
  - `pem.initialized` as initialization marker
- optional config file for model/backend selection
- local-model access through Ollama on Colossus

Minimum commands:
- `pemctl init`
- `pemctl create-ticket`
- `pemctl show-ticket`
- `pemctl update-ticket`
- `pemctl record-evidence`
- `pemctl propose-disposition`
- `pemctl accept`
- `pemctl reject`
- `pemctl defer`

That is enough for supervised governance without any CCE dependency.

**2. Smallest Useful End-To-End Workflow**

The smallest useful workflow is:

1. Human creates a ticket in PEM
2. PEM creates a case record
3. Work is performed locally on Colossus, by a human or local-model-assisted workflow
4. Evidence is attached to the case
5. PEM asks the operator for a disposition:
   - accept
   - reject
   - defer
6. PEM records the final state and rationale

That’s enough to supervise local-model work in a disciplined way.

Very small success definition:
- every ticket has a case id
- every case has explicit status
- every disposition says what was known, what was inferred, and what remains unverified

**3. Which Existing PEM Capabilities Are Actually Required**

Using the repo’s documented PEM role, these are the only capabilities I think are truly required at first:

1. **Decision continuity**
- one case record per ticket/work item
- stable case id
- timestamped updates

2. **Evidence recording**
- record source files, commands run, outputs, notes, and decision rationale

3. **Epistemic state labeling**
- explicitly mark:
  - known
  - inferred
  - unknown
  - deferred

4. **Disposition recording**
- accept
- reject
- defer

5. **Inspectable file output**
- JSON or Markdown records stored on disk, human-readable and Git-friendly

That is the minimum useful PEM.

**4. Which PEM Capabilities Can Be Deferred**

These can wait:

- CCE runtime integration
- automatic linking from Matrix messages into PEM
- hidden or ambient continuity
- workflow automation/orchestration
- multi-user review system
- permission model
- semantic retrieval over prior cases
- autonomous planning
- “smart” project decomposition
- policy engine sophistication
- front-end integration beyond CLI
- deep architecture formalization

Also deferrable:
- using PEM to govern every tiny interaction
- any attempt to make PEM the “brain” of Colossus

At first, PEM should be more like a **disciplined case notebook with operator commands**.

**5. What Local-Model Support Is Required From Colossus**

Minimum local-model support:

1. **Reliable Ollama runtime**
- local model callable from CLI
- predictable invocation path
- no frontier dependency required

2. **At least one usable local model for structured text work**
- good enough for:
  - summarizing evidence
  - drafting ticket updates
  - proposing disposition language
  - highlighting uncertainty

3. **Deterministic non-model path still available**
- PEM must work even if the model is unavailable
- the model should assist, not be required for basic governance

So required support is:
- Ollama installed
- one stable local text model
- CLI invocation from Colossus

Not required initially:
- tool calli

Here’s the corrected audit and deployment path, treating PEM as the intended backbone and CCE Lite as a separate communication/routing layer.

**Bottom line**

Based on this repo alone, PEM is **architecturally specified and materially evidenced**, but **not runnable here as a deployable code system**.

What exists today is not “nothing.” There is already:
- a defined PEM role and invariant set
- explicit PEM state surfaces
- rich PEM-shaped case artifacts with a nontrivial epistemic schema
- visible evidence that some PEM-producing system has existed or operated

What does **not** exist here is the deployable PEM runtime/codebase needed to run that architecture on Colossus.

So the shortest safe path is not “design a new PEM.”  
It is:

1. treat the current PEM docs + artifacts as authoritative signals of the chosen architecture  
2. identify the missing executable PEM implementation source  
3. bring that implementation onto Colossus as a separate system  
4. make it operate against the existing PEM state surfaces and invariants  
5. keep CCE Lite separate until both are stable

turns.answer:
**Colossus Layout**

Use this exact split:

- PEM code: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp`
- PEM governed root for first deployment: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root`
- Optional transitional kernel sibling, if you want full phase-1 kernel smoke: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test`

That keeps:
- code and venv in the PEM repo
- PEM state and config in a separate governed root
- CCE untouched and separate

**Runbook**

1. Sync PEM code from Black Air to Colossus.

```bash
rsync -av --delete \
  --exclude '.git/' \
  --exclude '.venv/' \
  --exclude '.pytest_cache/' \
  --exclude '.worktrees/' \
  --exclude '__pycache__/' \
  --exclude '*.pyc' \
  --exclude '.DS_Store' \
  --exclude '.env' \
  --exclude 'cases/' \
  --exclude 'scratch/' \
  --exclude 'stories/' \
  --exclude 'tickets/' \
  --exclude 'pem.initialized' \
  /Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/ \
  colossus:/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/
```

2. Create the governed root on Colossus.

```bash
ssh colossus 'mkdir -p /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root'
```

3. Seed governed-root config from the repo baseline.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cp /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/pem.toml /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml'
```

4. Create the Colossus `.env` in the PEM code repo.

Use `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/.env` with this content for first deployment:

```dotenv
PEM_MODE=prod
PEM_LLM_MODE=off
PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG=false
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root
```

Do not set `OPENAI_API_KEY` for this first milestone.

5. Install PEM on Colossus.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && python3 -m venv .venv && . .venv/bin/activate && python -m pip install --upgrade pip && pip install -r requirements.txt'
```

6. Start PEM manually for first validation.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml ./start.sh'
```

Expected success:
- it may print `Creating virtualenv...` or `Installing dependencies...` only on first run
- then it should stay attached silently, waiting on stdio
- no immediate traceback
- exit code should remain pending until the client disconnects

Expected failure:
- `python3 is required`
- `PEM bootstrap failed: ...`
- `PEM startup preflight failed: ...`
- traceback from `src/server.py`

7. Health-check the startup path without keeping the server attached.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml .venv/bin/python scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py'
```

Expected success:
- no output
- exit code `0`

Expected failure:
- `PEM startup preflight failed: initialize/tools.list failed: ...`
- or `PEM startup preflight failed: initialize succeeded but required tool pem_record is missing.`

8. Verify PEM state/persistence initialization.

```bash
ssh colossus 'ls -la /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root'
```

After first real PEM record/use, expect these to exist:
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/cases`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/scratch`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.initialized`

As PEM workflows run, expect:
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/stories`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/tickets`

9. Run the core acceptance test slice.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml .venv/bin/python -m unittest tests.test_project_root_resolver tests.test_project_init tests.test_project_init_integration tests.test_storage_modes tests.test_pem_case'
```

Expected success:
- `Ran ... tests in ...`
- `OK`

Expected failure:
- `FAILED (failures=..., errors=...)`

10. Run the full deterministic MCP smoke if the transitional kernel

**Status**

2. Colossus virtualenv/install did **not** succeed.

Exact failure:
```text
ERROR: Ignored the following versions that require a different python version: ... 1.26.0 Requires-Python >=3.10
ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement mcp==1.26.0 (from versions: none)
ERROR: No matching distribution found for mcp==1.26.0
```

Supporting diagnostic from Colossus:
```text
Python 3.9.6

Python 3.9.6

So the concrete blocker is:

3. `preflight_stdio_attach.py` was **not run** after the failed install.

- Install failure blocked smoke execution.

7. Errors / failures / unexpected behavior
- Primary failure: Python version mismatch on Colossus.
- Secondary surprise: the repo sync included far more build output than expected because `pem-touchid/.build` was present in the source tree.
- No PEM architecture changes were made.
- No CCE integration was attempted.
- No Ollama support work was attempted.

8. First deployment acceptance standard was **not met**.
- Reason: the PEM MCP server could not be installed on Colossus under the current default Python runtime.

This is a deployment-mechanics blocker, not a PEM architecture blocker.

The next smallest safe step would be to provision PEM on Colossus with Python `3.10+` and retry the same deployment flow unchanged. I have not taken that step yet.

turns.answer:
I re-ran the failing validation slice in a clean environment and compared it to the previous deployment-mode run.

**Conclusion**

This is **not just Colossus deployment env leakage** from `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root`.

It breaks into two buckets:

1. **Suite-order test environment leakage inside the PEM tests themselves**
2. **Separate real code/test incompatibilities that still fail even when env is clean**

**1. Clean rerun result**

I reran the same slice on Colossus with these unset:

- `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`
- `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`
- `PEM_MODE`
- `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE`

Command used:

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && . .venv/bin/activate && env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH -u PEM_MODE -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE python -m unittest tests.test_project_root_resolver tests.test_project_init tests.test_project_init_integration tests.test_storage_modes tests.test_pem_case'
```

Result:
- `Ran 176 tests`
- `FAILED (failures=3, errors=20, skipped=2)`

This matched the earlier failure pattern almost exactly.

So the original suspicion was only partly right:
- **not** deployment env leakage from the Colossus PEM runbook
- **yes** there is env leakage, but it is happening **inside the test suite**

**2. What is environment leakage vs real incompatibility**

**Environment leakage inside the suite**
The 20 `tests.test_pem_case` errors are consistent with stale `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT` values pointing at deleted tempdirs.

Why I’m confident:
- the error path is always an invalid temp directory like:
  - `/private/var/folders/.../tmp...`
- [`tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py) has many tests that call `reload_server(... storage_mode="standalone", storage_root=tempdir)`
- that helper sets a lot of env, but **does not clear `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`**
- later tests call `reload_server()` again, and server startup resolves the stale temp path after the tempdir has already been deleted

Relevant code:
- [`tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py)
- `reload_server()` sets `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`, `PEM_STORAGE_MODE`, `PEM_STORAGE_ROOT`, `PEM_KERNEL_PATH`
- but does **not** clear `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`

That explains the repeated errors:
```text
PEM startup bootstrap failed: PEM_PROJECT_ROOT points to an invalid workspace: /private/var/folders/.../tmp...
```

So those 20 errors are best classified as:
- **test harness / test isolation leakage**
- **not** evidence that the Colossus deployment env is wrong
- **not** proof that PEM stdio startup is broken in deployment mode

**Real incompatibility / drift**
Three failures remain even in isolation:

1. [`tests/test_project_init_integration.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py)
   - `test_init_disabled_skips_initialization`
   - failure:
   ```text
   AssertionError: True is not false
   ```

2. [`tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py)
   - `test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd`
   - failure:
   ```text
   STORAGE_ROOT resolved to tmp root, expected tmp/project_cases
   ```

3. [`tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py)
   - `test_clean_workspace_meta_status_uses_deterministic_defaults`
   - failure:
   ```text
   'env_pointer' != 'env'
   ```

I confirmed these are independent of deployment env by running them alone in a clean shell:

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && . .venv/bin/activate && env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH -u PEM_MODE -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE -u PEM_STORAGE_ROOT -u PEM_STORAGE_MODE -u PEM_KERNEL_PATH -u PEM_PROFILE -u PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG python -m unittest tests.test_project_init_integration.ProjectInitIntegrationTests.test_init_disabled_skips_initialization tests.test_storage_modes.StorageModeTests.test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd tests.test_storage_modes.Stora

turns.answer:
The narrow validation hardening pass is complete, and I did not change PEM runtime architecture or invariants.

What I changed:
- In [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py), I fixed the helper leakage by clearing stale `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`-related env before reload and preventing the deployment `.env` from contaminating ordinary unit imports.
- In [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py) and [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py), I applied the same test-mode isolation so repo-local deployment `.env` does not rewrite config expectations during unit tests.
- On Colossus, I synced the real transitional kernel checkout to `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test`, because `tests.test_pem_case` genuinely depends on that environment.

Diagnosis:
- The 20 tempdir bootstrap errors were caused by internal test leakage in `test_pem_case`, not PEM runtime behavior.
- The three isolated failures were caused by the deployed repo’s `.env` being loaded during tests, which made tests that expected “no config pointer” see `PEM_CONFIG_PATH` from deployment mode.
- The remaining `test_pem_case` failures after that were an environment issue on Colossus: the transitional kernel checkout was missing. I confirmed this by running a representative `test_pem_case` test on Black Air, where it passed.

On the three isolated failures:
- `test_init_disabled_skips_initialization`: stale test environment, not a PEM invariant problem. The deployed `.env` caused config-file mode to override the test’s env-only expectation.
- `test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd`: stale test environment, not runtime drift. Repo `.env` injected a config pointer, so cwd discovery never got a fair test.
- `test_clean_workspace_meta_status_uses_deterministic_defaults`: stale test environment, not runtime drift. `pem_config_source` reported `env_pointer` because `.env` had supplied `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`.

So the smallest valid fix was test-harness isolation, not runtime behavior change.

Reruns:
- Isolated 3 failing tests: `OK`
- `tests.test_pem_case`: `OK`
- Full 176-test slice: `OK`
- Deployment-mode `scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py`: `OK`

Exact clean test-mode command:
```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && . .venv/bin/activate && env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH -u PEM_MODE -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE -u PEM_STORAGE_ROOT -u PEM_STORAGE_MODE -u PEM_KERNEL_PATH -u PEM_PROFILE -u PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG python -m unittest tests.test_project_root_resolver tests.test_project_init tests.test_project_init_integration tests.test_storage_modes tests.test_pem_case'
```

Exact deployment-mode preflight command:
```bash

turns.answer:
I audited the smoke path/root mismatch, and the result is pretty clear:

- this is a **smoke harness / invocation issue**
- not a PEM runtime architecture defect
- not a reason to change PEM server behavior

**What the smoke expects**

During fixture replay, [`scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py) expects PEM to behave like the legacy phase-1 kernel façade:

- `pem_activate` should operate on the temporary copied kernel checkout
- ticket paths should resolve under:
  - `<temp kernel>/tickets/...`
- fixture stories should resolve under:
  - `<temp kernel>/stories/...`

That expectation is baked into:
- `load_fixtures()`
- `seed_ticket()`
- `run_fixture()`

**What PEM is actually doing in the smoke run**

In the current deployment-mode smoke invocation, PEM is starting in **standalone** mode.

That is why the latest smoke failure became:

```text
PEM_CONFIG_ERROR: Tickets are legacy-only in standalone mode.
```

This is actually internally consistent PEM behavior.

Why it happens:
- `smoke_mcp_phase1.py` starts the server with:
  - `cwd=str(REPO_ROOT)` where `REPO_ROOT` is `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp`
- server startup resolves `PROJECT_ROOT` from cwd fallback unless overridden
- then [`src/pem_config.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/src/pem_config.py) loads config from that repo root
- the repo’s [`pem.toml`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/pem.toml) says:
  - `storage.mode = "standalone"`

So the smoke harness is currently asking PEM to replay legacy kernel fixtures while also booting it in the repo’s standalone config context.

That mismatch is the real problem.

**Are deployment env vars causing this?**

Yes, partly, but not in the simplest way.

There are two distinct issues:

1. Earlier, deployment env plus repo `.env` caused stale smoke/tool assertions and some test contamination. We already fixed that on the test side.
2. Now, even after those fixes, the smoke still launches the server with:
   - repo cwd
   - repo-local config semantics
   - no forced legacy-kernel storage mode

So the key problem is less “deployment env leakage” and more:
- **the smoke invocation is using deployment-mode startup semantics for a fixture-semantic replay task**

That is the wrong execution context for this kind of smoke.

**Should smoke run in scrubbed fixture-test environment rather than deployment mode?**

Yes.

That is the main conclusion.

There should be **two different commands**:

1. **Deployment health check**
This is the one you already used and should keep:

```bash
cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root \
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml \
python scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py
```

Purpose:
- “Can the deployed PEM MCP server start correctly on Colossus?”

2. **Fixture/kernel semantic replay**
This should be a **scrubbed test-style invocation**, not deployment mode.

The smoke should run with:
- deployment env vars unset
- repo `.env` neutralized or bypassed
- explicit legacy-kernel mode forced
- `PEM_KERNEL_PATH` pointed at the temporary kernel or source kernel as appropriate

Conceptually, the smoke should be run more like:

```bash
cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT \
    -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH \
    -u PEM_MODE \
    -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE \
    -u PEM_STORAGE_ROOT \
    -u PEM_STORAGE_MODE \
    -u PEM_PROFILE \
    -u PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG \
    PEM_KERNEL_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test \
    PEM_STORAGE_MODE=legacy_kernel \
    python scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py
```

But there is one more important harness detail:

- the script’s internal `WorkingKernel.build_env()` should also explicitly enforce the legacy replay context for the spawned server process, instead of just copying parent env and adding `PEM_KERNEL_PATH`

Right now it only does:
- `PEM_KERNEL_PATH=<temp kernel>`
- proxy/network guards

turns.answer:
The smoke-harness-only fix worked.

What I changed:
- I updated [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py) only.
- No PEM runtime code changed.
- No `pem.toml` deployment semantics changed.
- No architecture or invariant changes were made.

What the smoke was doing wrong
- It was replaying legacy kernel fixtures while letting the spawned PEM server inherit repo/deployment semantics.
- That pulled PEM into standalone config behavior and also assumed older MCP reply shapes.
- The harness now correctly:
  - requires baseline phase-1 tools as a subset, not an exact set
  - reads the current standardized MCP envelopes under `result`
  - spawns the PEM server in a scrubbed legacy-kernel replay context
  - writes its own temporary `pem.toml` for replay
  - forces `PEM_STORAGE_MODE=legacy_kernel`
  - forces `PEM_KERNEL_PATH` to the temporary copied kernel
  - blocks deployment/root/config leakage from the spawned smoke server

```

- **Passed**

Governed-root state

- only `pem.toml` is present
- no `cases/`, `stories/`, `tickets/`, or `scratch/` were created by the fixture replay smoke

That is actually the right outcome:
- deployment health check and fixture semantic replay are now properly separated
- fixture replay stayed isolated from the real governed deployment root

**Acceptance**

Yes. I would now call the first PEM-on-Colossus deployment **accepted**.

Accepted scope:
- PEM installs on Colossus
- PEM MCP stdio startup works
- deployment-mode preflight passes
- full 176-test validation slice passes

Recommended commands going forward:

```bash

PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml \
python scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py
```

Fixture/kernel semantic replay:
```bash
cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root \
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml \
PEM_KERNEL_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test \
python scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py
```

Even though that second command includes deployment vars from the caller, the harness now scrubs and overrides the spawned server context correctly.

- `pg_wal/...` = PostgreSQL internal database write-ahead log
- `runtime/matrix-sync-state.json` = CCE’s live Matrix listener cursor/state

ELI-5:
- these are not the recipe
- these are the crumbs on the kitchen floor while the app is running

So this is why we do a dry run first: it showed us that a naive “copy everything” command is too blunt.

We do **not** want our normal sync command to push or delete:
- live database state
- runtime state
- other machine-specific junk

So the next step is: **make a safer rsync command**.

```bash
rsync -av --delete --dry-run \
  --exclude 'runtime/' \
  --exclude 'infra/matrix/state/' \
  --exclude '.git/' \
  /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce/ \
  colossus:/Users/kevinmattice/cce/
```

What these excludes mean:
- `runtime/` = don’t copy live bot state
- `infra/matrix/state/` = don’t copy live database/server state
- `.git/` = don’t copy Git internals this way

This is much closer to the safe workflow we want.

That tells us the answer: **this local repo has no GitHub remote configured yet**.

So Git isn’t refusing to push. It just doesn’t know **where** to push.

Think of it like this:
- your work is packed and ready
- but Git doesn’t have the mailing address yet

Because you previously mentioned [`kevinmattice/cognitive-environment`](https://github.com/kevinmattice/cognitive-environment), I’m assuming that is the GitHub repo you want this local repo connected to.

If that assumption is right, here’s the simplest path.

**Step 1: add the GitHub address**
Run this:

```bash
cd /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce
git remote add origin git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git
```

What this means:
- `origin` = nickname for your GitHub repo
- `git@github.com:...` = the SSH address of the repo

**Step 2: verify it stuck**
Then run:

```bash
git remote -v
```

You should now see something like:

```bash
origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (fetch)
origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (push)
```

**Step 3: push your local `master`**
If Step 2 looks right, run:

```bash
git push -u origin master
```

What this means:
- `push` = send your commits to GitHub
- `-u` = remember this remote/branch pairing so future pushes are easier
- `master` = your current branch

After that, future pushes are usually just:

```bash
git push
```

**One small caution**
This will work only if your Mac already has GitHub SSH access set up.

If it does not, GitHub may say something like:
- `Permission denied (publickey)`

If that happens, don’t worry. That would mean:
- the repo address is correct
- but your Mac hasn’t authenticated to GitHub yet

So let’s do this one step at a time.

Please run just these first two:

```bash
cd /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce
git remote add origin git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git
git remote -v
```

history.outcome:
Below is the shortest practical path I’d recommend from today’s CCE Lite state to a usable **PEM-on-Colossus** workflow.

The key reality check is this:

- **CCE Lite is real and working**
- **PEM is not implemented yet**
- the repo currently has **PEM role docs and case artifacts**, not a functioning PEM runtime

So the fastest path is not “finish PEM architecture.”  
It is: **build a very small supervised governance tool on Colossus that writes explicit records and uses the local model only where helpful.**

**1. Minimum Viable PEM Deployment On Colossus**

Minimum viable PEM should be a **local CLI tool plus file-backed state**, running beside CCE.

Smallest deployable form:
- a `pem/` tool directory on Colossus
- one CLI entrypoint, for example `pemctl`
- one durable state area, likely:
  - `cases/` for case records
  - `scratch/` for temporary working material
  - `pem.initialized` as initialization marker
- optional config file for model/backend selection
- local-model access through Ollama on Colossus

Minimum commands:
- `pemctl init`
- `pemctl create-ticket`
- `pemctl show-ticket`
- `pemctl update-ticket`
- `pemctl record-evidence`
- `pemctl propose-disposition`
- `pemctl accept`
- `pemctl reject`
- `pemctl defer`

That is enough for supervised governance without any CCE dependency.

**2. Smallest Useful End-To-End Workflow**

The smallest useful workflow is:

1. Human creates a ticket in PEM
2. PEM creates a case record
3. Work is performed locally on Colossus, by a human or local-model-assisted workflow
4. Evidence is attached to the case
5. PEM asks the operator for a disposition:
   - accept
   - reject
   - defer
6. PEM records the final state and rationale

That’s enough to supervise local-model work in a disciplined way.

Very small success definition:
- every ticket has a case id
- every case has explicit status
- every disposition says what was known, what was inferred, and what remains unverified

**3. Which Existing PEM Capabilities Are Actually Required**

Using the repo’s documented PEM role, these are the only capabilities I think are truly required at first:

1. **Decision continuity**
- one case record per ticket/work item
- stable case id
- timestamped updates

2. **Evidence recording**
- record source files, commands run, outputs, notes, and decision rationale

3. **Epistemic state labeling**
- explicitly mark:
  - known
  - inferred
  - unknown
  - deferred

4. **Disposition recording**
- accept
- reject
- defer

5. **Inspectable file output**
- JSON or Markdown records stored on disk, human-readable and Git-friendly

That is the minimum useful PEM.

**4. Which PEM Capabilities Can Be Deferred**

These can wait:

- CCE runtime integration
- automatic linking from Matrix messages into PEM
- hidden or ambient continuity
- workflow automation/orchestration
- multi-user review system
- permission model
- semantic retrieval over prior cases
- autonomous planning
- “smart” project decomposition
- policy engine sophistication
- front-end integration beyond CLI
- deep architecture formalization

Also deferrable:
- using PEM to govern every tiny interaction
- any attempt to make PEM the “brain” of Colossus

At first, PEM should be more like a **disciplined case notebook with operator commands**.

**5. What Local-Model Support Is Required From Colossus**

Minimum local-model support:

1. **Reliable Ollama runtime**
- local model callable from CLI
- predictable invocation path
- no frontier dependency required

2. **At least one usable local model for structured text work**
- good enough for:
  - summarizing evidence
  - drafting ticket updates
  - proposing disposition language
  - highlighting uncertainty

3. **Deterministic non-model path still available**
- PEM must work even if the model is unavailable
- the model should assist, not be required for basic governance

So required support is:
- Ollama installed
- one stable local text model
- CLI invocation from Colossus

Not required initially:
- tool calli

Here’s the corrected audit and deployment path, treating PEM as the intended backbone and CCE Lite as a separate communication/routing layer.

**Bottom line**

Based on this repo alone, PEM is **architecturally specified and materially evidenced**, but **not runnable here as a deployable code system**.

What exists today is not “nothing.” There is already:
- a defined PEM role and invariant set
- explicit PEM state surfaces
- rich PEM-shaped case artifacts with a nontrivial epistemic schema
- visible evidence that some PEM-producing system has existed or operated

What does **not** exist here is the deployable PEM runtime/codebase needed to run that architecture on Colossus.

So the shortest safe path is not “design a new PEM.”  
It is:

1. treat the current PEM docs + artifacts as authoritative signals of the chosen architecture  
2. identify the missing executable PEM implementation source  
3. bring that implementation onto Colossus as a separate system  
4. make it operate against the existing PEM state surfaces and invariants  
5. keep CCE Lite separate until both are stable

**Colossus Layout**

Use this exact split:

- PEM code: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp`
- PEM governed root for first deployment: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root`
- Optional transitional kernel sibling, if you want full phase-1 kernel smoke: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test`

That keeps:
- code and venv in the PEM repo
- PEM state and config in a separate governed root
- CCE untouched and separate

**Runbook**

1. Sync PEM code from Black Air to Colossus.

```bash
rsync -av --delete \
  --exclude '.git/' \
  --exclude '.venv/' \
  --exclude '.pytest_cache/' \
  --exclude '.worktrees/' \
  --exclude '__pycache__/' \
  --exclude '*.pyc' \
  --exclude '.DS_Store' \
  --exclude '.env' \
  --exclude 'cases/' \
  --exclude 'scratch/' \
  --exclude 'stories/' \
  --exclude 'tickets/' \
  --exclude 'pem.initialized' \
  /Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/ \
  colossus:/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/
```

2. Create the governed root on Colossus.

```bash
ssh colossus 'mkdir -p /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root'
```

3. Seed governed-root config from the repo baseline.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cp /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/pem.toml /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml'
```

4. Create the Colossus `.env` in the PEM code repo.

Use `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/.env` with this content for first deployment:

```dotenv
PEM_MODE=prod
PEM_LLM_MODE=off
PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG=false
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root
```

Do not set `OPENAI_API_KEY` for this first milestone.

5. Install PEM on Colossus.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && python3 -m venv .venv && . .venv/bin/activate && python -m pip install --upgrade pip && pip install -r requirements.txt'
```

6. Start PEM manually for first validation.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml ./start.sh'
```

Expected success:
- it may print `Creating virtualenv...` or `Installing dependencies...` only on first run
- then it should stay attached silently, waiting on stdio
- no immediate traceback
- exit code should remain pending until the client disconnects

Expected failure:
- `python3 is required`
- `PEM bootstrap failed: ...`
- `PEM startup preflight failed: ...`
- traceback from `src/server.py`

7. Health-check the startup path without keeping the server attached.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml .venv/bin/python scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py'
```

Expected success:
- no output
- exit code `0`

Expected failure:
- `PEM startup preflight failed: initialize/tools.list failed: ...`
- or `PEM startup preflight failed: initialize succeeded but required tool pem_record is missing.`

8. Verify PEM state/persistence initialization.

```bash
ssh colossus 'ls -la /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root'
```

After first real PEM record/use, expect these to exist:
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/cases`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/scratch`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.initialized`

As PEM workflows run, expect:
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/stories`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/tickets`

9. Run the core acceptance test slice.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml .venv/bin/python -m unittest tests.test_project_root_resolver tests.test_project_init tests.test_project_init_integration tests.test_storage_modes tests.test_pem_case'
```

Expected success:
- `Ran ... tests in ...`
- `OK`

Expected failure:
- `FAILED (failures=..., errors=...)`

10. Run the full deterministic MCP smoke if the transitional kernel

history.outcome:
The deployment stopped at the first real blocker, as requested.

**Status**

1. `rsync` succeeded.
- PEM repo synced to `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp` on Colossus.
- One operational surprise: the source repo included a large built `pem-touchid/.build` tree and a `pem-conversion-test` symlink, so the sync was much heavier than the minimal plan assumed.
- Final transfer completed successfully.

2. Colossus virtualenv/install did **not** succeed.
- Failure occurred during `pip install -r requirements.txt` in [`/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/requirements.txt`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/requirements.txt).

Exact failure:
```text
ERROR: Ignored the following versions that require a different python version: ... 1.26.0 Requires-Python >=3.10
ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement mcp==1.26.0 (from versions: none)
ERROR: No matching distribution found for mcp==1.26.0
```

Supporting diagnostic from Colossus:
```text
Python 3.9.6
/usr/bin/python3
Python 3.9.6
```

So the concrete blocker is:
- Colossus is using `python3` = `Python 3.9.6`
- PEM requires `mcp==1.26.0`
- that package requires Python `>= 3.10`

3. `preflight_stdio_attach.py` was **not run** after the failed install.
- I stopped before attempting a broken preflight.

4. The governed root was created correctly.
- Created: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root`
- Seeded config: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml`
- Wrote Colossus `.env`: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/.env`

**Conclusion**

This is **not just Colossus deployment env leakage** from `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root`.

It breaks into two buckets:

1. **Suite-order test environment leakage inside the PEM tests themselves**
2. **Separate real code/test incompatibilities that still fail even when env is clean**

**1. Clean rerun result**

I reran the same slice on Colossus with these unset:

- `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`
- `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`
- `PEM_MODE`
- `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE`

Command used:

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && . .venv/bin/activate && env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH -u PEM_MODE -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE python -m unittest tests.test_project_root_resolver tests.test_project_init tests.test_project_init_integration tests.test_storage_modes tests.test_pem_case'
```

Result:
- `Ran 176 tests`
- `FAILED (failures=3, errors=20, skipped=2)`

This matched the earlier failure pattern almost exactly.

So the original suspicion was only partly right:
- **not** deployment env leakage from the Colossus PEM runbook
- **yes** there is env leakage, but it is happening **inside the test suite**

**2. What is environment leakage vs real incompatibility**

**Environment leakage inside the suite**
The 20 `tests.test_pem_case` errors are consistent with stale `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT` values pointing at deleted tempdirs.

Why I’m confident:
- the error path is always an invalid temp directory like:
  - `/private/var/folders/.../tmp...`
- [`tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py) has many tests that call `reload_server(... storage_mode="standalone", storage_root=tempdir)`
- that helper sets a lot of env, but **does not clear `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`**
- later tests call `reload_server()` again, and server startup resolves the stale temp path after the tempdir has already been deleted

Relevant code:
- [`tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py)
- `reload_server()` sets `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`, `PEM_STORAGE_MODE`, `PEM_STORAGE_ROOT`, `PEM_KERNEL_PATH`
- but does **not** clear `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`

That explains the repeated errors:
```text
PEM startup bootstrap failed: PEM_PROJECT_ROOT points to an invalid workspace: /private/var/folders/.../tmp...
```

So those 20 errors are best classified as:
- **test harness / test isolation leakage**
- **not** evidence that the Colossus deployment env is wrong
- **not** proof that PEM stdio startup is broken in deployment mode

**Real incompatibility / drift**
Three failures remain even in isolation:

   - `test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd`
   - failure:
   ```text
   STORAGE_ROOT resolved to tmp root, expected tmp/project_cases
   ```

3. [`tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py)
   - `test_clean_workspace_meta_status_uses_deterministic_defaults`
   - failure:
   ```text

   ```

I confirmed these are independent of deployment env by running them alone in a clean shell:

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && . .venv/bin/activate && env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH -u PEM_MODE -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE -u PEM_STORAGE_ROOT -u PEM_STORAGE_MODE -u PEM_KERNEL_PATH -u PEM_PROFILE -u PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG python -m unittest tests.test_project_init_integration.ProjectInitIntegrationTests.test_init_disabled_skips_initialization tests.test_storage_modes.StorageModeTests.test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd tests.test_storage_modes.Stora

What I changed:
- In [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py), I fixed the helper leakage by clearing stale `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`-related env before reload and preventing the deployment `.env` from contaminating ordinary unit imports.
- In [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py) and [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py), I applied the same test-mode isolation so repo-local deployment `.env` does not rewrite config expectations during unit tests.
- On Colossus, I synced the real transitional kernel checkout to `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test`, because `tests.test_pem_case` genuinely depends on that environment.

Diagnosis:
- The 20 tempdir bootstrap errors were caused by internal test leakage in `test_pem_case`, not PEM runtime behavior.
- The three isolated failures were caused by the deployed repo’s `.env` being loaded during tests, which made tests that expected “no config pointer” see `PEM_CONFIG_PATH` from deployment mode.
- The remaining `test_pem_case` failures after that were an environment issue on Colossus: the transitional kernel checkout was missing. I confirmed this by running a representative `test_pem_case` test on Black Air, where it passed.

On the three isolated failures:
- `test_init_disabled_skips_initialization`: stale test environment, not a PEM invariant problem. The deployed `.env` caused config-file mode to override the test’s env-only expectation.
- `test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd`: stale test environment, not runtime drift. Repo `.env` injected a config pointer, so cwd discovery never got a fair test.
- `test_clean_workspace_meta_status_uses_deterministic_defaults`: stale test environment, not runtime drift. `pem_config_source` reported `env_pointer` because `.env` had supplied `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`.

So the smallest valid fix was test-harness isolation, not runtime behavior change.

history.outcome:
I audited the smoke path/root mismatch, and the result is pretty clear:

- this is a **smoke harness / invocation issue**
- not a PEM runtime architecture defect
- not a reason to change PEM server behavior

**What the smoke expects**

During fixture replay, [`scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py) expects PEM to behave like the legacy phase-1 kernel façade:

- `pem_activate` should operate on the temporary copied kernel checkout
- ticket paths should resolve under:
  - `<temp kernel>/tickets/...`
- fixture stories should resolve under:
  - `<temp kernel>/stories/...`

That expectation is baked into:
- `load_fixtures()`
- `seed_ticket()`
- `run_fixture()`

**What PEM is actually doing in the smoke run**

In the current deployment-mode smoke invocation, PEM is starting in **standalone** mode.

That is why the latest smoke failure became:

```text
PEM_CONFIG_ERROR: Tickets are legacy-only in standalone mode.
```

This is actually internally consistent PEM behavior.

Why it happens:
- `smoke_mcp_phase1.py` starts the server with:
  - `cwd=str(REPO_ROOT)` where `REPO_ROOT` is `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp`
- server startup resolves `PROJECT_ROOT` from cwd fallback unless overridden
- then [`src/pem_config.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/src/pem_config.py) loads config from that repo root
- the repo’s [`pem.toml`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/pem.toml) says:
  - `storage.mode = "standalone"`

So the smoke harness is currently asking PEM to replay legacy kernel fixtures while also booting it in the repo’s standalone config context.

That mismatch is the real problem.

**Are deployment env vars causing this?**

Yes, partly, but not in the simplest way.

There are two distinct issues:

1. Earlier, deployment env plus repo `.env` caused stale smoke/tool assertions and some test contamination. We already fixed that on the test side.
2. Now, even after those fixes, the smoke still launches the server with:
   - repo cwd
   - repo-local config semantics
   - no forced legacy-kernel storage mode

So the key problem is less “deployment env leakage” and more:
- **the smoke invocation is using deployment-mode startup semantics for a fixture-semantic replay task**

That is the wrong execution context for this kind of smoke.

**Should smoke run in scrubbed fixture-test environment rather than deployment mode?**

Yes.

That is the main conclusion.

There should be **two different commands**:

1. **Deployment health check**
This is the one you already used and should keep:

```bash
cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root \
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml \
python scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py
```

Purpose:
- “Can the deployed PEM MCP server start correctly on Colossus?”

2. **Fixture/kernel semantic replay**
This should be a **scrubbed test-style invocation**, not deployment mode.

The smoke should run with:
- deployment env vars unset
- repo `.env` neutralized or bypassed
- explicit legacy-kernel mode forced
- `PEM_KERNEL_PATH` pointed at the temporary kernel or source kernel as appropriate

Conceptually, the smoke should be run more like:

```bash
cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT \
    -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH \
    -u PEM_MODE \
    -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE \
    -u PEM_STORAGE_ROOT \
    -u PEM_STORAGE_MODE \
    -u PEM_PROFILE \
    -u PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG \
    PEM_KERNEL_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test \
    PEM_STORAGE_MODE=legacy_kernel \
    python scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py
```

But there is one more important harness detail:

- the script’s internal `WorkingKernel.build_env()` should also explicitly enforce the legacy replay context for the spawned server process, instead of just copying parent env and adding `PEM_KERNEL_PATH`

Right now it only does:
- `PEM_KERNEL_PATH=<temp kernel>`
- proxy/network guards

- No PEM runtime code changed.

What the smoke was doing wrong

  - blocks deployment/root/config leakage from the spawned smoke server

Even though that second command includes deployment vars from the caller, the harness now scrubs and overrides the spawned server context correctly.

That output is actually very helpful.

Those 2 files are:

- `runtime/matrix-sync-state.json`

Here’s the important part: those are **not normal code files**.

They are more like “live machine state” files:
- `pg_wal/...` = PostgreSQL internal database write-ahead log
- `runtime/matrix-sync-state.json` = CCE’s live Matrix listener cursor/state

ELI-5:
- these are not the recipe
- these are the crumbs on the kitchen floor while the app is running

So this is why we do a dry run first: it showed us that a naive “copy everything” command is too blunt.

We do **not** want our normal sync command to push or delete:
- live database state
- runtime state
- other machine-specific junk

So the next step is: **make a safer rsync command**.

Use this dry run next:

```bash
rsync -av --delete --dry-run \
  --exclude 'runtime/' \
  --exclude 'infra/matrix/state/' \
  --exclude '.git/' \
  /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce/ \
  colossus:/Users/kevinmattice/cce/
```

What these excludes mean:
- `runtime/` = don’t copy live bot state
- `infra/matrix/state/` = don’t copy live database/server state
- `.git/` = don’t copy Git internals this way

This is much closer to the safe workflow we want.

- `rsync` is what copies files to Colossus for runtime
- these are two separate things

ELI-5:
- `git push` = save your work to GitHub
- `sync-colossus` = copy your work to the live machine

So you do **not** push from Colossus.

**The simplest next step**

```bash
cd /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce
git remote -v
```

- shows whether this repo already knows where GitHub is

origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (fetch)
origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (push)

turns.answer:
That tells us the answer: **this local repo has no GitHub remote configured yet**.

So Git isn’t refusing to push. It just doesn’t know **where** to push.

Think of it like this:
- your work is packed and ready
- but Git doesn’t have the mailing address yet

Because you previously mentioned [`kevinmattice/cognitive-environment`](https://github.com/kevinmattice/cognitive-environment), I’m assuming that is the GitHub repo you want this local repo connected to.

If that assumption is right, here’s the simplest path.

**Step 1: add the GitHub address**
Run this:

```bash
cd /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce
git remote add origin git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git
```

What this means:
- `origin` = nickname for your GitHub repo
- `git@github.com:...` = the SSH address of the repo

**Step 2: verify it stuck**
Then run:

```bash
git remote -v
```

You should now see something like:

```bash
origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (fetch)
origin  git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git (push)
```

**Step 3: push your local `master`**
If Step 2 looks right, run:

```bash
git push -u origin master
```

What this means:
- `push` = send your commits to GitHub
- `-u` = remember this remote/branch pairing so future pushes are easier
- `master` = your current branch

After that, future pushes are usually just:

```bash
git push
```

**One small caution**
This will work only if your Mac already has GitHub SSH access set up.

If it does not, GitHub may say something like:
- `Permission denied (publickey)`

If that happens, don’t worry. That would mean:
- the repo address is correct
- but your Mac hasn’t authenticated to GitHub yet

So let’s do this one step at a time.

Please run just these first two:

```bash
cd /Volumes/kevinmattice/cce
git remote add origin git@github.com:kevinmattice/cognitive-environment.git
git remote -v
```

Paste the output, and then I’ll walk you through the push itself.

turns.answer:
Below is the shortest practical path I’d recommend from today’s CCE Lite state to a usable **PEM-on-Colossus** workflow.

The key reality check is this:

- **CCE Lite is real and working**
- **PEM is not implemented yet**
- the repo currently has **PEM role docs and case artifacts**, not a functioning PEM runtime

So the fastest path is not “finish PEM architecture.”  
It is: **build a very small supervised governance tool on Colossus that writes explicit records and uses the local model only where helpful.**

**1. Minimum Viable PEM Deployment On Colossus**

Minimum viable PEM should be a **local CLI tool plus file-backed state**, running beside CCE.

Smallest deployable form:
- a `pem/` tool directory on Colossus
- one CLI entrypoint, for example `pemctl`
- one durable state area, likely:
  - `cases/` for case records
  - `scratch/` for temporary working material
  - `pem.initialized` as initialization marker
- optional config file for model/backend selection
- local-model access through Ollama on Colossus

Minimum commands:
- `pemctl init`
- `pemctl create-ticket`
- `pemctl show-ticket`
- `pemctl update-ticket`
- `pemctl record-evidence`
- `pemctl propose-disposition`
- `pemctl accept`
- `pemctl reject`
- `pemctl defer`

That is enough for supervised governance without any CCE dependency.

**2. Smallest Useful End-To-End Workflow**

The smallest useful workflow is:

1. Human creates a ticket in PEM
2. PEM creates a case record
3. Work is performed locally on Colossus, by a human or local-model-assisted workflow
4. Evidence is attached to the case
5. PEM asks the operator for a disposition:
   - accept
   - reject
   - defer
6. PEM records the final state and rationale

That’s enough to supervise local-model work in a disciplined way.

Very small success definition:
- every ticket has a case id
- every case has explicit status
- every disposition says what was known, what was inferred, and what remains unverified

**3. Which Existing PEM Capabilities Are Actually Required**

Using the repo’s documented PEM role, these are the only capabilities I think are truly required at first:

1. **Decision continuity**
- one case record per ticket/work item
- stable case id
- timestamped updates

2. **Evidence recording**
- record source files, commands run, outputs, notes, and decision rationale

3. **Epistemic state labeling**
- explicitly mark:
  - known
  - inferred
  - unknown
  - deferred

4. **Disposition recording**
- accept
- reject
- defer

5. **Inspectable file output**
- JSON or Markdown records stored on disk, human-readable and Git-friendly

That is the minimum useful PEM.

**4. Which PEM Capabilities Can Be Deferred**

These can wait:

- CCE runtime integration
- automatic linking from Matrix messages into PEM
- hidden or ambient continuity
- workflow automation/orchestration
- multi-user review system
- permission model
- semantic retrieval over prior cases
- autonomous planning
- “smart” project decomposition
- policy engine sophistication
- front-end integration beyond CLI
- deep architecture formalization

Also deferrable:
- using PEM to govern every tiny interaction
- any attempt to make PEM the “brain” of Colossus

At first, PEM should be more like a **disciplined case notebook with operator commands**.

**5. What Local-Model Support Is Required From Colossus**

Minimum local-model support:

1. **Reliable Ollama runtime**
- local model callable from CLI
- predictable invocation path
- no frontier dependency required

2. **At least one usable local model for structured text work**
- good enough for:
  - summarizing evidence
  - drafting ticket updates
  - proposing disposition language
  - highlighting uncertainty

3. **Deterministic non-model path still available**
- PEM must work even if the model is unavailable
- the model should assist, not be required for basic governance

So required support is:
- Ollama installed
- one stable local text model
- CLI invocation from Colossus

Not required initially:
- tool calli

turns.answer:
Here’s the corrected audit and deployment path, treating PEM as the intended backbone and CCE Lite as a separate communication/routing layer.

**Bottom line**

Based on this repo alone, PEM is **architecturally specified and materially evidenced**, but **not runnable here as a deployable code system**.

What exists today is not “nothing.” There is already:
- a defined PEM role and invariant set
- explicit PEM state surfaces
- rich PEM-shaped case artifacts with a nontrivial epistemic schema
- visible evidence that some PEM-producing system has existed or operated

What does **not** exist here is the deployable PEM runtime/codebase needed to run that architecture on Colossus.

So the shortest safe path is not “design a new PEM.”  
It is:

1. treat the current PEM docs + artifacts as authoritative signals of the chosen architecture  
2. identify the missing executable PEM implementation source

turns.answer:
**Colossus Layout**

Use this exact split:

- PEM code: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp`
- PEM governed root for first deployment: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root`
- Optional transitional kernel sibling, if you want full phase-1 kernel smoke: `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test`

That keeps:
- code and venv in the PEM repo
- PEM state and config in a separate governed root
- CCE untouched and separate

**Runbook**

1. Sync PEM code from Black Air to Colossus.

```bash
rsync -av --delete \
  --exclude '.git/' \
  --exclude '.venv/' \
  --exclude '.pytest_cache/' \
  --exclude '.worktrees/' \
  --exclude '__pycache__/' \
  --exclude '*.pyc' \
  --exclude '.DS_Store' \
  --exclude '.env' \
  --exclude 'cases/' \
  --exclude 'scratch/' \
  --exclude 'stories/' \
  --exclude 'tickets/' \
  --exclude 'pem.initialized' \
  /Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/ \
  colossus:/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/
```

2. Create the governed root on Colossus.

```bash
ssh colossus 'mkdir -p /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root'
```

3. Seed governed-root config from the repo baseline.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cp /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/pem.toml /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml'
```

4. Create the Colossus `.env` in the PEM code repo.

Use `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp/.env` with this content for first deployment:

```dotenv
PEM_MODE=prod
PEM_LLM_MODE=off
PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG=false
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root
```

Do not set `OPENAI_API_KEY` for this first milestone.

5. Install PEM on Colossus.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && python3 -m venv .venv && . .venv/bin/activate && python -m pip install --upgrade pip && pip install -r requirements.txt'
```

6. Start PEM manually for first validation.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml ./start.sh'
```

Expected success:
- it may print `Creating virtualenv...` or `Installing dependencies...` only on first run
- then it should stay attached silently, waiting on stdio
- no immediate traceback
- exit code should remain pending until the client disconnects

Expected failure:
- `python3 is required`
- `PEM bootstrap failed: ...`
- `PEM startup preflight failed: ...`
- traceback from `src/server.py`

7. Health-check the startup path without keeping the server attached.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml .venv/bin/python scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py'
```

Expected success:
- no output
- exit code `0`

Expected failure:
- `PEM startup preflight failed: initialize/tools.list failed: ...`
- or `PEM startup preflight failed: initialize succeeded but required tool pem_record is missing.`

8. Verify PEM state/persistence initialization.

```bash
ssh colossus 'ls -la /Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root'
```

After first real PEM record/use, expect these to exist:
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/cases`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/scratch`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.initialized`

As PEM workflows run, expect:
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/stories`
- `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/tickets`

9. Run the core acceptance test slice.

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml .venv/bin/python -m unittest tests.test_project_root_resolver tests.test_project_init tests.test_project_init_integration tests.test_storage_modes tests.test_pem_case'
```

Expected success:
- `Ran ... tests in ...`
- `OK`

Expected failure:
- `FAILED (failures=..., errors=...)`

10. Run the full deterministic MCP smoke if the transitional kernel

**Status**

1. `rsync` succeeded.

2. Colossus virtualenv/install did **not** succeed.

Exact failure:
```text
ERROR: Ignored the following versions that require a different python version: ... 1.26.0 Requires-Python >=3.10
ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement mcp==1.26.0 (from versions: none)
ERROR: No matching distribution found for mcp==1.26.0
```

Supporting diagnostic from Colossus:
```text
Python 3.9.6
/usr/bin/python3
Python 3.9.6
```

So the concrete blocker is:
- Colossus is using `python3` = `Python 3.9.6`

3. `preflight_stdio_attach.py` was **not run** after the failed install.

6. `smoke_mcp_phase1.py` was **skipped**.
- Install failure blocked smoke execution.

7. Errors / failures / unexpected behavior
- Primary failure: Python version mismatch on Colossus.
- Secondary surprise: the repo sync included far more build output than expected because `pem-touchid/.build` was present in the source tree.
- No PEM architecture changes were made.
- No CCE integration was attempted.
- No Ollama support work was attempted.

8. First deployment acceptance standard was **not met**.
- Reason: the PEM MCP server could not be installed on Colossus under the current default Python runtime.

**What this means**

This is a deployment-mechanics blocker, not a PEM architecture blocker.

The next smallest safe step would be to provision PEM on Colossus with Python `3.10+` and retry the same deployment flow unchanged. I have not taken that step yet.

turns.answer:
I re-ran the failing validation slice in a clean environment and compared it to the previous deployment-mode run.

**Conclusion**

This is **not just Colossus deployment env leakage** from `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root`.

It breaks into two buckets:

1. **Suite-order test environment leakage inside the PEM tests themselves**
2. **Separate real code/test incompatibilities that still fail even when env is clean**

**1. Clean rerun result**

I reran the same slice on Colossus with these unset:

- `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`
- `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`
- `PEM_MODE`
- `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE`

Command used:

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && . .venv/bin/activate && env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH -u PEM_MODE -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE python -m unittest tests.test_project_root_resolver tests.test_project_init tests.test_project_init_integration tests.test_storage_modes tests.test_pem_case'
```

Result:
- `Ran 176 tests`
- `FAILED (failures=3, errors=20, skipped=2)`

This matched the earlier failure pattern almost exactly.

So the original suspicion was only partly right:
- **not** deployment env leakage from the Colossus PEM runbook
- **yes** there is env leakage, but it is happening **inside the test suite**

**2. What is environment leakage vs real incompatibility**

**Environment leakage inside the suite**
The 20 `tests.test_pem_case` errors are consistent with stale `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT` values pointing at deleted tempdirs.

Why I’m confident:
- the error path is always an invalid temp directory like:
  - `/private/var/folders/.../tmp...`
- [`tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py) has many tests that call `reload_server(... storage_mode="standalone", storage_root=tempdir)`
- that helper sets a lot of env, but **does not clear `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`**
- later tests call `reload_server()` again, and server startup resolves the stale temp path after the tempdir has already been deleted

Relevant code:
- [`tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py)
- `reload_server()` sets `PEM_CONFIG_PATH`, `PEM_STORAGE_MODE`, `PEM_STORAGE_ROOT`, `PEM_KERNEL_PATH`
- but does **not** clear `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`

That explains the repeated errors:
```text
PEM startup bootstrap failed: PEM_PROJECT_ROOT points to an invalid workspace: /private/var/folders/.../tmp...
```

So those 20 errors are best classified as:
- **test harness / test isolation leakage**
- **not** evidence that the Colossus deployment env is wrong
- **not** proof that PEM stdio startup is broken in deployment mode

**Real incompatibility / drift**
Three failures remain even in isolation:

1. [`tests/test_project_init_integration.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py)
   - `test_init_disabled_skips_initialization`
   - failure:
   ```text
   AssertionError: True is not false
   ```

2. [`tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py)
   - `test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd`
   - failure:
   ```text
   STORAGE_ROOT resolved to tmp root, expected tmp/project_cases
   ```

3. [`tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py)
   - `test_clean_workspace_meta_status_uses_deterministic_defaults`
   - failure:
   ```text
   'env_pointer' != 'env'
   ```

I confirmed these are independent of deployment env by running them alone in a clean shell:

```bash
ssh colossus 'cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp && . .venv/bin/activate && env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH -u PEM_MODE -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE -u PEM_STORAGE_ROOT -u PEM_STORAGE_MODE -u PEM_KERNEL_PATH -u PEM_PROFILE -u PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG python -m unittest tests.test_project_init_integration.ProjectInitIntegrationTests.test_init_disabled_skips_initialization tests.test_storage_modes.StorageModeTests.test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd tests.test_storage_modes.Stora

turns.answer:
The narrow validation hardening pass is complete, and I did not change PEM runtime architecture or invariants.

What I changed:
- In [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_pem_case.py), I fixed the helper leakage by clearing stale `PEM_PROJECT_ROOT`-related env before reload and preventing the deployment `.env` from contaminating ordinary unit imports.
- In [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_project_init_integration.py) and [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/tests/test_storage_modes.py), I applied the same test-mode isolation so repo-local deployment `.env` does not rewrite config expectations during unit tests.
- On Colossus, I synced the real transitional kernel checkout to `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test`, because `tests.test_pem_case` genuinely depends on that environment.

Diagnosis:
- The 20 tempdir bootstrap errors were caused by internal test leakage in `test_pem_case`, not PEM runtime behavior.
- The three isolated failures were caused by the deployed repo’s `.env` being loaded during tests, which made tests that expected “no config pointer” see `PEM_CONFIG_PATH` from deployment mode.
- The remaining `test_pem_case` failures after that were an environment issue on Colossus: the transitional kernel checkout was missing. I confirmed this by running a representative `test_pem_case` test on Black Air, where it passed.

On the three isolated failures:
- `test_init_disabled_skips_initialization`: stale test environment, not a PEM invariant problem. The deployed `.env` caused config-file mode to override the test’s env-only expectation.
- `test_project_local_config_discovery_uses_cwd`: stale test environment, not runtime drift. Repo `.env` injected a config pointer, so cwd discovery never got a fair test.

turns.answer:
I audited the smoke path/root mismatch, and the result is pretty clear:

- this is a **smoke harness / invocation issue**
- not a PEM runtime architecture defect
- not a reason to change PEM server behavior

**What the smoke expects**

During fixture replay, [`scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py) expects PEM to behave like the legacy phase-1 kernel façade:

- `pem_activate` should operate on the temporary copied kernel checkout
- ticket paths should resolve under:
  - `<temp kernel>/tickets/...`
- fixture stories should resolve under:
  - `<temp kernel>/stories/...`

That expectation is baked into:
- `load_fixtures()`
- `seed_ticket()`
- `run_fixture()`

**What PEM is actually doing in the smoke run**

In the current deployment-mode smoke invocation, PEM is starting in **standalone** mode.

That is why the latest smoke failure became:

```text
PEM_CONFIG_ERROR: Tickets are legacy-only in standalone mode.
```

This is actually internally consistent PEM behavior.

Why it happens:
- `smoke_mcp_phase1.py` starts the server with:
  - `cwd=str(REPO_ROOT)` where `REPO_ROOT` is `/Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp`
- server startup resolves `PROJECT_ROOT` from cwd fallback unless overridden
- then [`src/pem_config.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/src/pem_config.py) loads config from that repo root
- the repo’s [`pem.toml`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/pem.toml) says:
  - `storage.mode = "standalone"`

So the smoke harness is currently asking PEM to replay legacy kernel fixtures while also booting it in the repo’s standalone config context.

That mismatch is the real problem.

**Are deployment env vars causing this?**

Yes, partly, but not in the simplest way.

There are two distinct issues:

1. Earlier, deployment env plus repo `.env` caused stale smoke/tool assertions and some test contamination. We already fixed that on the test side.
2. Now, even after those fixes, the smoke still launches the server with:
   - repo cwd
   - repo-local config semantics
   - no forced legacy-kernel storage mode

So the key problem is less “deployment env leakage” and more:
- **the smoke invocation is using deployment-mode startup semantics for a fixture-semantic replay task**

That is the wrong execution context for this kind of smoke.

**Should smoke run in scrubbed fixture-test environment rather than deployment mode?**

Yes.

That is the main conclusion.

There should be **two different commands**:

1. **Deployment health check**
This is the one you already used and should keep:

```bash
cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root \
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml \
python scripts/preflight_stdio_attach.py
```

Purpose:
- “Can the deployed PEM MCP server start correctly on Colossus?”

2. **Fixture/kernel semantic replay**
This should be a **scrubbed test-style invocation**, not deployment mode.

The smoke should run with:
- deployment env vars unset
- repo `.env` neutralized or bypassed
- explicit legacy-kernel mode forced
- `PEM_KERNEL_PATH` pointed at the temporary kernel or source kernel as appropriate

Conceptually, the smoke should be run more like:

```bash
cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
env -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT \
    -u PEM_CONFIG_PATH \
    -u PEM_MODE \
    -u PEM_PROJECT_ROOT_SOURCE \
    -u PEM_STORAGE_ROOT \
    -u PEM_STORAGE_MODE \
    -u PEM_PROFILE \
    -u PEM_ALLOW_GLOBAL_CONFIG \
    PEM_KERNEL_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test \
    PEM_STORAGE_MODE=legacy_kernel \
    python scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py
```

But there is one more important harness detail:

- the script’s internal `WorkingKernel.build_env()` should also explicitly enforce the legacy replay context for the spawned server process, instead of just copying parent env and adding `PEM_KERNEL_PATH`

Right now it only does:
- `PEM_KERNEL_PATH=<temp kernel>`
- proxy/network guards
turns.q: Yep — the date looks right, and the duplicate bullet is the thing to clean up next.

turns.answer:
The smoke-harness-only fix worked.

What I changed:
- I updated [`/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py`](/Users/kevinmattice/dev/anon/pem-mcp/scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py) only.
- No PEM runtime code changed.
- No `pem.toml` deployment semantics changed.
- No architecture or invariant changes were made.

What the smoke was doing wrong
- It was replaying legacy kernel fixtures while letting the spawned PEM server inherit repo/deployment semantics.
- That pulled PEM into standalone config behavior and also assumed older MCP reply shapes.
- The harness now correctly:
  - requires baseline phase-1 tools as a subset, not an exact set
  - reads the current standardized MCP envelopes under `result`
  - spawns the PEM server in a scrubbed legacy-kernel replay context
  - writes its own temporary `pem.toml` for replay
  - forces `PEM_STORAGE_MODE=legacy_kernel`
  - forces `PEM_KERNEL_PATH` to the temporary copied kernel
  - blocks deployment/root/config leakage from the spawned smoke server

1. `scripts/smoke_mcp_phase1.py`
- **Passed**

```

Governed-root state

That is actually the right outcome:
- deployment health check and fixture semantic replay are now properly separated
- fixture replay stayed isolated from the real governed deployment root

**Acceptance**

Yes. I would now call the first PEM-on-Colossus deployment **accepted**.

Accepted scope:

- PEM MCP stdio startup works

PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root \

```

Fixture/kernel semantic replay:

cd /Users/kevinmattice/pem-mcp
. .venv/bin/activate
PEM_PROJECT_ROOT=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root \
PEM_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-governed-root/pem.toml \
PEM_KERNEL_PATH=/Users/kevinmattice/pem-conversion-test \

Even though that second command includes deployment vars from the caller, the harness now scrubs and overrides the spawned server context correctly.
